text.title: Nine Minimum Product Frameworks
text.summary: The nine minimum-product frameworks, each mapped to its purpose and primary use condition.

rule.statement: Choose the framework before scoping. The choice determines what "minimum" means for your release.
rule.rationale: Each framework optimises for a different success criterion. Defaulting to MVP in a crowded consumer market produces a product users ignore.

list.item: MVP — Minimum Viable Product: test core assumptions. Use when entering a new market with an unvalidated idea.
MLP — Minimum Lovable Product: create emotional connection. Use in crowded markets where differentiation is experience.
MMP — Minimum Marketable Product: generate first revenue. Use in B2B and enterprise sales contexts.
MAC — Minimum Awesome Component: build one killer feature. Use when feature-based differentiation is the strategy.
RAT — Riskiest Assumption Test: validate the biggest risk. Use on high-uncertainty projects before any build.
MDP — Minimum Delightful Product: exceed expectations. Use for premium positioning.
MVA — Minimum Valuable Product: deliver business value to the buyer. Use in enterprise ROI-focused contexts.
MFP — Minimum Functional Product: prove technical feasibility. Use for complex infrastructure or novel tech.
SLC — Simple, Lovable, Complete: balance simplicity and polish. Use for consumer apps needing immediate full-loop value.

rule.statement: In crowded markets, an MVP that is merely "viable" will fail. Users already have "good enough" solutions; you need "awesome" or "lovable" to earn a switch.
rule.rationale: Market density changes the minimum bar. A blue-ocean product can be rough; a red-ocean product must clear an existing quality floor before users will try it.
